title: QuTech Launches Open-Architecture Tuna-17 Superconducting Processor on Quantum Inspire Cloud Platform - Quantum Computing Report
gist: QuTech (TU Delft + TNO) put its 17-qubit, 24-tunable-coupler superconducting Tuna-17 processor on the free Quantum Inspire cloud, its third system in 12 months, built by an all-European multi-vendor consortium as a sovereignty-focused open-architecture alternative to closed commercial quantum stacks ahead of a 28-qubit successor.

insights.claim: QuTech deployed Tuna-17, a 17-superconducting-qubit processor with 24 tunable couplers, on the Quantum Inspire public cloud with free, un-capped access and up to 100,000 shots per batch.
insights.speaker: Mohamed Abdel-Kareem
insights.so_what: Free public access to live superconducting hardware lowers the barrier for researcher and student adoption, seeding a European developer base outside IBM/Google walled gardens.
insights.quote: Free public access via Quantum Inspire; up to 100,000 shots per batch.

insights.claim: Tuna-17 is the third system release within a 12-month cycle, succeeding Tuna-5 and Tuna-9 and establishing a baseline before the larger 28-qubit Tuna-28.
insights.speaker: Mohamed Abdel-Kareem
insights.so_what: A three-generation cadence in a year signals fast iteration velocity for a public research consortium; Tuna-28 is the next milestone to watch.
insights.quote: The launch represents the third system release within a 12-month development cycle
insights.locator: Accessible globally through the Quantum Inspire public cloud

insights.claim: The DiCarlo Lab design uses tunable couplers to dynamically adjust inter-qubit coupling frequencies, suppressing parasitic spectator effects and crosstalk during parallel gates, as detailed in a Physical Review Letters paper on frequency positioning of tunable couplers.
insights.speaker: Mohamed Abdel-Kareem
insights.so_what: Crosstalk suppression via tunable couplers is a concrete engineering advance toward surface-code error correction, the gating problem for useful superconducting QC.
insights.quote: Optimizing the Frequency Positioning of Tunable Couplers in a Circuit QED Processor to Mitigate Spectator Effects on Quantum Operations
insights.locator: The Architecture of the Tuna-17 Processor

insights.claim: The processor supports a universal gate set, automated self-calibration, real-time performance logging, and mid-circuit measurements, and integrates with Qiskit and PennyLane so users can run hybrid NISQ algorithms without modifying existing codebases.
insights.speaker: Mohamed Abdel-Kareem
insights.so_what: Mid-circuit measurement plus mainstream SDK support makes the machine a credible platform for error-correction research, not just demos.
insights.locator: The processing unit supports a universal gate set

insights.claim: Unlike closed, vertically integrated commercial quantum stacks, the Tuna platform enforces a strict open-architecture standard across an entirely European value chain.
insights.speaker: Mohamed Abdel-Kareem
insights.so_what: An open, multi-vendor architecture is a deliberate strategic bet against the proprietary full-stack model of US incumbents, aiming to become an industry-standard component layer.
insights.quote: the Tuna platform enforces a strict open-architecture standard across its entire European value chain
insights.locator: Unlike closed, vertically integrated commercial quantum computing stacks

insights.claim: System integration is partitioned across a Delft cluster of named suppliers: QuTech/TU Delft (QPU design, fabrication, SDK), TNO (micro-compilation, QPU geometry), Orange Quantum Systems (quantum OS and calibration software), Qblox (microwave control electronics), Delft Circuits (cryogenic RF cabling), and QuantWare (traveling-wave parametric amplifiers and sub-Kelvin packaging).
insights.speaker: Mohamed Abdel-Kareem
insights.so_what: This is a full map of the Delft quantum supply chain; several vendors (Qblox, QuantWare) are also Ground State Ventures portfolio names, showing how concentrated and investable the ecosystem is.

insights.claim: Tuna-17 is financed partly through the HectoQubit consortium under Quantum Delta NL Phase 2 CAT-1 and serves as a hardware demonstrator for the EU OpenSuperQPlus Flagship, aiming to secure European technological sovereignty in semiconductor-adjacent manufacturing.
insights.speaker: Mohamed Abdel-Kareem
insights.so_what: State and EU funding tying quantum to sovereignty parallels chip-industry policy; implies continued public capital and procurement supporting these European vendors.
insights.quote: This approach ensures European technological sovereignty in semiconductor-adjacent manufacturing
insights.locator: Financed in part through the HectoQubit consortium
